title: Logic & Operators Quiz 🏆
description: Prove your conditional mastery! Math, equality, booleans, and if-else statements.

questions.question: What is the result of 15 % 4 in Dart?
questions.explanation: 4 goes into 15 three times (making 12). There are 3 left over.

questions.question: Which operator is used to check if two things are exactly equal?
questions.explanation: Double equals == checks for equality. Single = is for assigning a value.

questions.question: If you want to subtract exactly 1 from a variable named `hp`, you can write: hp__;
questions.explanation: The decrement operator -- subtracts exactly 1.

questions.question: The result of Dart division (/) is always a double, even if the result is a whole number like 10 / 2.
questions.explanation: In Dart, the / operator always returns a double. To get integer division, use `~/`.

questions.question: How do you check if a variable `score` is NOT equal to 0?
questions.explanation: The exclamation mark followed by equals (!=) means 'not equal'.

questions.question: An `if` statement block will execute if the condition evaluates to either `true` or `1`.
questions.explanation: Dart does not treat '1' as true. The condition inside an 'if' must strictly evaluate to a `bool` (true or false).

questions.question: Which of these correctly writes an if/else block?
questions.explanation: Dart syntax wraps the condition in () and the code block in {}.

questions.question: What happens if an `if` condition is false, and there is no `else` block?
options: Dart simply skips the block and continues running the rest of the code.
questions.explanation: If there is no 'else', Dart just ignores the 'if' block entirely when the condition is false.

questions.question: Fill in the blank to chain decisions: else __ (...)
questions.explanation: Dart uses 'else if' to chain conditional checks.

questions.question: What keyword acts as the catch-all inside a 'switch' statement if no cases match?
questions.explanation: The 'default:' case is executed if none of the specific 'case' variations matched the variable.

questions.question: You must include a `break;` statement at the end of non-empty `case` blocks in a switch.
questions.explanation: If you don't use 'break;', Dart will throw an error to prevent accidental fallthrough execution.

questions.question: In a Ternary Operator (Condition ? A : B), when does 'B' execute?
questions.explanation: The ternary format is: Condition ? ValueIfTrue : ValueIfFalse.

questions.question: What is the output of: `print( 10 >= 10 );`
questions.explanation: 10 is equal to 10, so greater-than-OR-EQUAL-to is true.

questions.question: What is the symbol for Greater Than?
questions.explanation: The > symbol points to the right and means greater than.

questions.question: The ternary operator is identical in behavior to a very simple if-else statement.
questions.explanation: Yes, it is just syntactic sugar for a standard, simple if-else assignment.

questions.question: If x=5, what is x after running: `x++;`
questions.explanation: x++ means x = x + 1, so 5 changes to 6.

questions.question: Which statement best describes when to use `switch`?
options: When checking complex math ranges like > 50 and < 100
When checking one specific variable against many exact, discrete values
When you need to loop over an array of data
questions.explanation: Switch is best for matching a single variable against exact discrete choices (like status codes or days of week).

questions.question: The assignment operator `=` can be used inside an if condition, like `if (score = 100)`.
questions.explanation: No, that's an assignment. Dart requires conditions to be strictly `bool`, so you must use `==`.

questions.question: Complete the logic: `bool isEven = (num % 2 == 0);` What does this do?
options: Checks if `num` is divisible by 2 with no remainder, assigning true if it is an even number.
Divides num by 2 and saves it in isEven.
questions.explanation: Using modulo 2 ( % 2 ) checking for 0 remainder is the standard way to check for even numbers.

questions.question: To add 5 to the existing value of `score`, you can use the shortcut: score _ 5;
questions.explanation: The += operator adds the right value to the existing variable.
